title: 2026/04/02 - EN (Part 3)
displayTitle: Environment Committee - Environment (Disestablishment of Ministry for the Environment) Amendment Bill [Part 3]
topic: Environment (Disestablishment of Ministry for the Environment) Amendment Bill
description: Thursday 2 April 2026 | Environment Committee Environment (Disestablishment of Ministry for the Environment) Amendment Bill Hearing of evidence: List of submitters: Animal
descriptionSummary: Environment (Disestablishment of Ministry for the Environment) Amendment Bill

chunks.text: We are in New Zealand and we can see you there, Danette. Welcome to the Select Committee. Look, you have five minutes with us, so we'll hand it over to you to talk us through your submission. Thank you. Thank you. So, Tēnā koutou katoa. Thank you for the opportunity to speak. My name is Jeheta Danette Wedderter. I'm representing the Animal Justice Party Aotearoa, New Zealand. We are a political party committed to advocating for the rights, welfare and protection of animals. So we oppose the Environment Disestablishment Amendment Bill, but firstly, I will acknowledge the government's intent to make improvements across housing, infrastructure and the environment. They genuinely do have complex challenges and integrated thinking does have real value. So this proposal is just not

an administration restructure. It is a decision about how environmental protection is prioritised within government and whether it remains a clear, independent voice. So removing that independence and placing those responsibilities within the broader ministry does create tension. Addressing housing and infrastructure challenges cannot come at the expense of environmental protection. So you're asking the same ministry to build the motorway and protect the wetlands that the motorway destroys. That's not coordination, that's a conflict of interest written into an org chart. These portfolios are not neutral. They are among the primary drivers of habitat destruction, freshwater and ecosystem decline. So the government has suggested that this change will improve coordination.

However, coordination already occurs and can be strengthened with process. So disestablishing a dedicated ministry is not required to achieve that. As an experienced senior leader, a cannabis structural change does cost time and a lot of money and doesn't fix problems. I recommend identifying the root cause of the issues and providing solutions to address them. Now, importantly, environmental protection and animal welfare are inseparable. So animals rely on stable ecosystems for survival. That's their food, shelter, breeding and safety. And when these systems are weakened, it's animals who pay the price. We've already seen this. Aotearoa is facing ongoing biodiversity loss and environmental pressure. And globally, we know that ecosystem decline is one of the leading

drivers of species decline. And there are broader implications. So New Zealand's reputation is tightly close to how our environment, how we care for our environment. And that reputation underpins trust in our clean green billion dollar image. Equally important is accountability. So a standalone ministry provides a clear line of responsibility when things go wrong. So 70% of freshwater fish are threatened with extinction or at risk. 78% of native lamb birds and 93% of native frogs and 94% of native reptiles are threatened or at risk. Nearly half of our monitored river sites fail to meet E. coli safe drinking water standards. And half of our lakes are in poor or degraded health due to nutrient contamination.

The ministry has been responsible for bringing this kind of clear evidence-based reporting to light. And when that voice is absorbed, there is a real risk of this level of transparency and visibility being diminished over time. So we urge you to protect this proposal. And instead of broad structural change, the focus should be on targeted practical improvements that strengthen environmental outcomes. We have a responsibility to do better. Thank you. Thank you very much, Dineet, for your submission. Appreciate your time. It appears we have no questions, but we have got your written submission as well. It's very clear submission. Yeah, very clear, very passionate. So we appreciate your time this morning. Thank you.

Please, Kia ora. Kia ora. So we will now move to Ginny Easton, who I believe is joining us. Oh, there we go. Ginny, we can see you there. Welcome to the select committee this morning. Look, you've got five minutes with us. So in the next time, we'll hand it over to you to talk us through your submission. Thank you. I began my written submission by explaining how important MFE's technical knowledge, independent voice and international standing were for me, a resource scientist with Tasman District Council, which is a small unitary authority, covering mainly rural land

and the raft of complex contaminated historic sites. MFE scientists established the contaminated sites remediation fund for the cleanup of the Mapua fruit grows chemical site. They also provided nationwide technical advice across and access to high temperature incineration in France through the Basel Convention for the disposal of hazardous waste such as DDT, dealdron, PCBs, from the redundant agrochemical collection. And without this technical support from MFE, farmers and industries would not have supported the strive by councils to remove toxic waste for liability issues. The parameters for environmental reporting are complex.

Ozone bioprotection, waste minimization, all require special technical knowledge which is provided by MFE staff. Now a question, would this still be available if MFE staff were buried inside a mega ministry? And I seriously doubt that for the following reasons. A well funded standalone ministry has a louder, stronger, more authoritative voice and it can take environmental concerns to the cabinet table. And this makes it possible to recruit and retain high quality staff and preserve the status of the ministry, build institutional knowledge and with the status and professional relationships have access to overseas experts for the emerging

issues. Those unknowns which are arriving here soon. And this status and manner will be lost if the secretary for the environment functions are given to the CEO of the mega ministry. And then there's the climate work. The staff at MFE already have a battle to provide advice and policies to meet our climate responsibilities for emission reduction. When the climate commission's advice is not accepted and the ETS has been ordered in such a way it's no longer effective. We need to move to a low carbon economy and protect the carbon sinks and we need the ministry staff to guide us. Work on climate adaptation plan is very complex and politically

challenging and with the process and with the actual process of merging the ministry that will take the staff's focus away from these projects and without a clear national directive. Local and central government staff are managing the recovery from climatic weather events in a very ad hoc manner. And as you know this is unsustainable. It's very expensive and it's inherently unfair. MFE is far too important for our future to merge and weaken its voice for nature and our physical and natural environment. We need its independent oversight to support, protect and improve our economy and well-being. And as a last note I hope that the NEB and

planning bills are changed to include a goal for emission reduction targets, goals and plans under the Climate Change Response Act 2002. If this doesn't happen the role of MFE will be even more challenging and difficult. If they're part of a mega ministry where their voice will be diluted as they compete with transport and urban development that are finished. Thank you very much Jenny for your submission today. We appreciate your time and the effort that you've made to provide that submission. So thank you, thank you very much. Thank you. It's very helpful. Very helpful. Good point about the screening.

Thank you. Thank you Jenny. So now we'll move on to our next submission today, Bruce Gilkerson. So we've just got Bruce popping up. There we go. Bruce, welcome to the select committee this morning. You have five minutes with us so we'll hand it over to you to open up with some opening remarks and talk you through your submission. Talk us through your submission. We just got

something to share with us there. Yes, brilliant. Thank you. Over to you. Okay, here and thank you very much. I am with Paul Martin. So my background is in business and accounting. His is in health and disability and he'll speak if I get tongue tied. We will say we disagree with the establishment proposal but if that is to proceed we will have some suggestions in relation to how that option might be improved. So that's the second slide we're looking at there. I also do some research and writing on environmental issues mainly for chartered accountants in New Zealand and Australia for their monthly journal. The image is an early involvement from 26 years ago. I've included it here for the strong cross-party support it

attracted. It was launched by Sir Jeffrey Palmer and included a foreword from Simon Upton who was then Minister for the Environment. I'm pleased to say that climate and nature are now a central focus for chartered accountants Australia and New Zealand. Next slide please. Talking about urgency, this is the planetary boundaries framework which many of you will be familiar with, largely developed by scientists at the Stockholm Resilience Centre. Seven of the nine safe global limits have now been transgressed so it's urgent. Climate is one of the boundaries despite some progress recently the rate of emissions globally is still speeding up. A few comments as we now know the past 11 years are the hottest on record.

Everything is connected some impacts cause feedback loops resulting in further damage elsewhere. So heat can lead to forest fires and dieback which releases carbon which leads to more heat. Limits once breached can be irreversible and meanwhile every fraction of a of a degree really matters. Next the globally trusted UK based institute and faculty of actuaries using methods tried and trusted to manage and advise on trillions of dollars in pensions, investments and insurance has concluded that risks to planetary solvency have been hugely

underestimated and that without rapid action on climate and environmental risks the global economy could face a 50% loss in GDP and yes that's 50% between 2070 and 2090. So they're saying it's even worse than we thought sorry and that better information clear limits and better policies are urgently needed everywhere. The report is a call to action and is highly recommended. Next please. Here I'm saying that extra costs to retain MFE's independence and I don't know what they are or indeed what the benefits are will be hugely offset by environmental

and other benefits. So first there is ample evidence of potential gains and I focused here mainly on dollars not just on environmental benefits. Here are two sources for now. Modeling by Deloitte's New Zealand showed that decisive climate action could add 64 billion to our economy by 2050 and a report from the Sustainable Business Council and Climate Leaders Coalition this week shows that climate friendly technology could see GDP grow by 22 billion per year by 2035. It could also help prevent declines in agriculture and tourism

revenue. Some other benefits I've noted here are savings from mitigating and preparing wisely for future climate damage avoiding possible reputational damage and risks to free trade agreements from inadequate emissions progress and avoiding some future significant health costs. For each of those it's better and cheaper to protect something rather than try to fix it later. MFE's independence expertise clearly a purpose and ability to act and advise transparently available and needed like never before. Slide please. I'm wondering if MFE is to merge is

the new ministry the right place for it and I'm just looking at impacts and benefits from two major two major blocks. So question is where could that expertise provide most benefit and on this basis though I'm not recommending this there could potentially be greater benefits in aligning with the latter. I consider that the merger should certainly proceed but without MFE and at least not with more detailed research into the benefits and risks for current and future New Zealanders. Slide please. This one's a bit flippant but I'm just talking about the

the benefits of having a independent an independent voice so yeah rather than have be the an environment agency in one section but to better to act transparently to be able to advise encourage and guide all sectors and particularly those which are larger matters have significant impacts or are presenting major opportunities for progress and better outcomes. Next please. I mentioned the heading which probably should have been headed items to be addressed if the bill proceeds and if we do this how do we make

sure that nothing gets lost rather than the heading amendments because some of these are outside the bill itself for instance some items are covered by the rma reforms currently underway. The focus on the status of the secretary for the environment's role in the priority of MFE is increasingly urgent climate and environmental responsibilities are key concerns. It's crucial that nothing diminishes or detracts from these and there be no loss of momentum which through what could be a turbulent period if the full merger were to occur as proposed as noted I consider that the retention of MFE as an independent ministry is likely to bring

about far better outcomes for people on planet. And final slide please. With that one looks as if it got lost a bit at the top there but with multiple and escalating impacts now inevitable this is the wrong time to risk weakening a clear and focused and independent voice for the environment. I consider that even with some refinement on the proposal unless compelling evidence is available to the contrary a change at this stage could result in disruption dilution and diminished transparency. Meanwhile retaining and bolstering this voice in my view

is the best way to mitigate and prepare for negative impacts and to plan for NC's significant future opportunities. New Zealanders current and future love and depend on their natural environment for their livelihoods and their well-being. Please support them. We welcome any questions. Thank you Bruce and thank you for those slides that's really useful and helpful. Look we've got a couple of questions um we'll go to Priyanka first. Thank you thanks Bruce um I've just got a quick question seeking clarification around the first amendment that you have suggested to explicitly prioritize environment protection as a primary function if this merger were to go ahead. Other submitters have made the point

that they feel that even though the bill as it stands says that all the functions would transfer over to the new ministry that the focus on the natural environment might fall away as a result. So presumably you are talking about prioritizing both the natural and built environments in that amendment that you've suggested? Yes um I wanted to make sure that it's it's um uh it's protected and bolstered. There are a lot of threats coming the the next few years are going to be um perhaps quite too moltuous and certainly not wanting anything to be lost there. Thank you. Thank you and Rachel Brookham.

Oh yes just a quick question thank you um for the history and for your involvement all that time ago. Um and you're I'm interested in the international experience you have and you've just said I believe it is just a matter of time before we are challenged by countries and producers which are now far more environmentally focused than we are. What what countries do you think are far more environmentally focused now than New Zealanders? Yes I'm probably referring there to to to UK and EU which if certainly they have a strong focus for instance in our free trade agreements on not backing off on on climate progress and I think it could

perhaps farmers in those areas might might raise concerns um and yeah I think any any further changes there could put us at risk. Thank you. William well thank you very much Bruce we've met time there with our submissions this morning but we appreciate your time and the um the really insightful useful submissions so thank you. Thank you. Like the pictures the pictures of the all blacks in the I don't know if we won that one yeah thank you. I thought it was going to be that game with France. Oh yes. So moving on to our next submission this morning uh Environment and

Conservation Organisations of Aotearoa New Zealand. Who are also joining us online and hopefully popping through shortly. There we go we can oh there you can see you there Barry welcome to the select committee this afternoon look you have 10 minutes with us so we'll hand it over to you to talk us through your submission and then we'll open up for a few questions. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you to the committee um to um present uh this our submission um I'm assuming you've had a

read our submission already I introduced myself I'm Barry Weber I'm the chairperson of the Environment Conservation Organisations um as you've seen in our submission AECO's had a long history and involvement in this area we were involved in advocacy for the establishment of the ministry for the environment back in the 1980s but for those who don't know that came out of the Environment Forum which was established in 1985 where a number of issues clearly identified with the lack of environmental priority of the governments at that stage and the failures that the think big projects had occurred um and the costs and losses that were associated with that with those projects on the environment. In addition the lack of focus um

on environmental issues generally and the idea that we need to bring in together all the different green dots in the different agencies at the time and the commission for the environment as it then was was only a agency with a cabinet minute that didn't have any statutory functions or you know statutory basis for its decision making um so the Environment Act was a really important part of that and I should note that this is the 40th year of the Environment Act that was it was passed in December of 1986 so the Ministry for the Environment uh if it still exists at the end of the year would have reached its 40th year so that's I think it's important milestone in environment policy in New Zealand that we should not forget. So we're concerned about

the lack of environmental focus you've heard from other submitters this morning about that and we're concerned about the lack of environmental focus of the government generally and the need for recognition that the environment is a key important part of the uh of of New Zealand in terms of our loss of biodiversity our state of the environment which is declining and we need to have a lot greater focus on it and we're concerned that this piece of legislation would make and weaken that environmental focus. We'll also note that yesterday the government released its priorities for a science funding which also shows a huge reduction in environmental science funding and again that doesn't augur well for the future of New Zealand's environment or

the making the government and other agencies making clear decisions on the environment because science funding is a key part of that equation. We're also concerned that we're talking about this currently talking about this bill but the new position has been appointed the head of corrections has been appointed to this position of running an agency which parliament is yet to pass legislation to enforce so it's a bit of an odd way of doing things and we hope that this is an open and transparent process that parliament is going through and the executive is an overriding parliamentary it's parliamentary mandate in that process. We're also concerned that the cabinet papers associated with this document and this decision are heavily redacted

and I don't know where the parliament itself has actually looked at the full detail of those cabinet papers or asked for copies of the unredacted copies of those papers that were released and we urge the select committee to ask for those papers so that they can review all the documentation and the reasons for the decisions being made. The other problem is that the legislation itself misses part of the role of the ministry for the environment in different agencies. I haven't mentioned to it but I would suggest that the drafters look at the Fisheries Act for example as one as one led piece of legislation which mentions the ministry for the environment which isn't included and so there are there are potentially other examples

we haven't looked through the full details but there clearly the drafters of this bill was done in a hurry without clear thought and consideration of all the references to the environment agency in different pieces of legislation. We're concerned about the transparency of advice that will be with this new agency. We've seen in other agencies where there's been accumulation of different ministries for example ministry for primary industry we've noticed the lack of transparency in terms of some fisheries advice and the way that occurs within an agency and the sort of priorities for funding within that agency so it's really important that whatever comes out of this process that there's clear transparency between the

different parts if it is combined the different parts of the agency so we can see where officials disagree or officials agree and what priorities different parts of the agency have especially when they're being acting under different statutes. That's a sort of a sort of clear summary of our submission we don't support this bill we urge the select committee to not to pass not to recommend this bill be passed and that in fact that we move forward and reconsider the role of the ministry for the environment in the future. Thank you chair and happy to answer any questions that the committee may have in regard to our submission thanks.

Thank you Barry we'll go first to Rachel Brokking. Yeah thanks Barry and thanks for reminding us of nationals blueprint cleaning up fresh water and the like. I often go back to that document it does seem rather inconsistent with everything that we've seen before this committee this term but my question for you is more specifically on seven where you talk about when the Environment Act was passed that there was supposed to be environmental impact assessment provisions and where you see that best sitting into all of the environmental scheme also noting that we've got the planning bill and the natural environment bill in front of us too. Well I don't know whether the select committee have heard of the environmental protection and enhancement

procedures probably not they were established in the late 70s and revised during the 80s they were wider than environmental assessment for projects they had had had provisions for assessment of policies and actions of agencies rather than just actual consent conditions so while the resource management act might be argued replace some of those provisions of the environmental protection enhancement procedures not all the provisions of those procedures were replaced and I'd urge the select committee to get yourself a copy of the EPNEP procedures I'm assuming that they'll be in parliamentary library they're not something that you can find online I actually looked online to see whether I could

find them but you can't these days they're actually a very forward looking piece of document they were originally produced under the national government in the Muldoon years and were revised later on through through the 1980s and the original intention of the Environment Act was that basically part three of the Environment Act would contain the environmental protection enhancement procedures but that never occurred there was never despite earlier decisions by cabinet to do that that was never proceeded with and I think that's a great admission because as I said the Resource Management Act and other legislation only for full full part of the provisions that the EPNEP procedures actually covered thanks

thank you thank you very much Barry we appreciate your submission today and your insights lots of considerations to take into account there so thank you very much thank you thank you thank you committee look forward to your decisions thanks so now we move on to our next submitter the Pirirako Tribal Authority who are here with us in person welcome welcome to the select committee this afternoon now look you've got 10 minutes with us but take your submission as read and if you'd like to just talk us three main points and then we'll open up for a few questions so over to you

thank you I'm speaking on behalf of Pirirako Tribal Authority of Val Kaihautu for generations Pirirako hapu have been a kaitaki of our here in the west of may of plenty and our connection with the whenua with our waters is more than just cultural this is fundamental to our very identity and survival so I'm here to be confirmed opposition to the environment this establishment of the Ministry of the Environment Amendment Bill review this bill not just as a bureaucratic reshuffle as has

been suggested but as a direct calculated attack on Maori rights and the protection of Aotearoa's greatest asset our natural environment. Tuatahiaki the bill is in breach of Article 2 of the Treaty of Waitangi which guarantees us Tino Rangatiratanga over our taonga more specifically to us of the Pirirako the bill threatens to hollow out the Ngāhapū o Ngāti Ranginui claims Settlement Act 2025 that settlement was quote a living promise from the government on behalf of the Crown to acknowledge the past wrongs and protect our taonga for future generations the Crown apologised for the unjust confiscation of our lands and promised a new relationship based

on mutual trust yet here we are less than a year later watching the government unilaterally without any external consultation with treaty partners getting ready to dismantle the very agency responsible for upholding those relationships and protecting the after mentioned taonga a commitment made must be a commitment kept you can't deliver on treaty settlements if you scatter the expertise and relationships required to honour them we question the logic of merging ministries that appear to have minimal intersecting areas of mutual alignment we also question the sense and value of having a mega ministry in the first place these are entities keeping a mind

that are tasked with overseeing the most important areas in our society throwing them together to make a mega ministry seems like a knee jerk reaction to save money in the short term when speaking of the justification of the merge of several ministries I've seen terms like improve coordination be used terms like align policy making across sectors terms like reduce duplication and bureaucracy this is political speak for downsizing downsizing several different areas into one in this case ministries results always in a dilution of expertise and ultimately a reduction in financial support we can't do that for the environment it's too important

to do that having priorities such as economic development and environmental protections competing with each other within the same ministry weakens the environment of both of them and while we speak of economic development and financial responsibility defunding and deprioritizing the environment doesn't save us money in the long term it just pushes the bill that we all ultimately have to pay down the road for example cyclone Gabrielle needed over 10 billion dollars of financial support for resilience and recovery the earthquakes and kai kota billions of dollars needed for recovery and resilience the 2023 floods in Auckland billions

of dollars needed for recovery and resilience and those are just some examples we live within the ecosystem here in Altaroa that is heavily reliant on meat exports dairy exports fruit exports that need a functioning not collapsing environment tourism agriculture fisheries depend entirely on clean water and a healthy environment for us a pediraco water culture is a major source of income and worry about what the space will look like in the absence of proactive environmental leadership from our government and from our ministries saving millions now was to only cost us billions later to me

it's not sound economic strategy it's physically irresponsible and there's much more at stake here of course than just money we enter pediraco we share our space with the moana and there's grave concern for our urupaa and our people in the case of a major weather event I am the environment the environment is me this proverb doesn't only encapsulate the relationship Maori have with the environment but also a pathway forward where we can both thrive and so I'll conclude my portal with the question of the government

finds itself in this situation pushing against long-term sustainability pushing against environmental protections and pushing against long-term economic sound decision making then I will question the wisdom of that position and I will just conclude by saying the pediraco tribal authority stand in opposition of this bill thank you very much we've got a question from Cameron Luxton yeah thank you you too um I've got a question you talked about saying quote was um it's hollowing out the Nati Dunginui Kirao settlement bill um the relationship with the crown like is the settlement with the crown or the ministry because I look through this

yeah so looking through the settlement bill there's no mention of ministry for the environment but the ministry of the environment is the entity that oversees and ultimately looks after the protections and that relationship or diminishing of the ministry in our view ultimately results in the diminishing of the environment which is where that conflict occurs so would the crown ever be able to change the makeup of ministries and not breach the Nati Dunginui settlement bill of course but this is ultimately again as we said ultimately this bill will result and as I said a dilution of expertise and a reduction of funds for the environment and that has an impact

yeah so it's up coming to me on this point so I think your point is can we the question is am I am I all wrong with what I'm saying is that all these issues on your back page here at your bill go to the diminishment of environmental protections and it is because of that as a consequence of the MFE um being abolished that go to your that go to your settlement issue is that is that right the ultimate point is that a diminishing of the ministry diminishes the capabilities to look after the environment and by diminishing the capabilities to look after the environment and our ability to look after our time that ultimately is the conflict

yeah which is much more eloquently put than my question thank you thank you very much for your time today appreciate your submission and and your passion um as well so thank you a lot of that and so we now call our next submitter wwf new zealand who are also here and joining us in person today welcome to this select committee this afternoon you have five minutes with us and so we'll just hand it over to you to open up and talk us through your submission and then we'll have hopefully a few questions great kere kouta thank you for the opportunity to speak to you all today um i'm jamie fowler and the conservation impact manager at wwf new zealand and i'm here with lily and fujer the principle policy advisor

and we understand that the spell doesn't change the core functions of the ministry for the environment but we are concerned about how those functions will operate inside a much broader new ministry bringing environment together with strong development housing transport and infrastructure mandates risks creating built-in conflicts that could weaken the ministry's ability to carry out its core environmental protection role new zealand's biodiversity is already in crisis um with some of the highest rates of extinction in the world driven by habitat loss pollution and cumulative impacts the ministry for the environment currently provides essential safeguards for nature those safeguards cannot be diluted by competing goals like road construction or urban expansion as we know nature underpins our health our well-being our climate resilience

and our economy primary industries and tourism rely heavily on healthy ecosystems and if the environmental mandate is weakened those foundations erode and future generations will bear that cost the environment act requires the ministry to consider things like ecosystem health threatened species landscapes areas of importance to tanga to finoa pollution natural hazards hazardous substances and cumulative effects these are not optional extras they are core stewardship duties we are concerned that within a much larger ministry these functions would be overshadowed by the development priorities the bill doesn't provide clarity or safeguards to show how these tensions will be managed without explicit direction environmental protection risks becoming secondary

we also challenge the assumption that this merger will create efficiency combining such different mandates blurs organizational purpose confuses advice to ministers shifts conflicts to inside one agency rather than resolving them between clear specialized ministries this is likely to slow decisions advice and internal negotiations far from creating efficiency it risks managing making the system less effective than what we have now ww of new zealand does not support the bill or the merger as proposed however if the merger proceeds we strongly recommend three things first strengthen the bill so environmental functions cannot be weakened through secondary legislation or organizational restructuring environmental protection biodiversity

and ecosystem health must be explicitly prioritized for the new priorities for the new ministry's purpose and powers second ensure that the secretary for the environment keeps an independent authoritative role able to give free and frank environmental advice supported by staff who have genuine environmental expertise and experience this is particularly important in the context of the rma replacement work which will take very many years to enact and implement third improve transparency and accountability the annual reporting statutory environmental limits and independent oversight must be strong enough to prevent uh environmental outcomes being compromised to conclude al teodos environment is globally unique and under immense pressure it needs strong clear institutional guardianship and we urge the committee to ensure the bill

and any related instruments explicitly protect the integrity of the environmental mandate within the new ministry thank you for your time and we're happy to answer any queries thank you very much for that submission uh pre anchor here's the first question thank you um you've mentioned in your written submission your concerns around um the conflicts between development and environmental protection which seems to me to be quite similar to the trade-offs that pc has mentioned in his submission um would you go so far as he has to say that you would suggest that mfe's left out of the measure i would i think the transparency and visibility of those trade-offs is incredibly important um the reason why mfe was uh you know established in the first place was to drive the quality

uh decision making and informed decision making and evidence um to fully understand those impacts of our decisions on the environment and that that still remains an important function just use one of them yeah um so when you say there needs to be visibility part of what he has said is that uh the trade-offs could be then the risk is that the trade-offs would be managed within this mega ministry and not elevated that's right two ministers and that's what you mean by that yes the contestable advice needs to be yeah thank you so thank you very much for your submission today we appreciate those considerations so thank you thank you so we now move to our next submitter uh troy baseman

troy welcome to this committee this afternoon uh you have five minutes with us so uh we'll hand it over to you to talk us through your submission thank you tenakota tenakota katawa um thanks for hearing this um i um am an environmental scientist uh working across science and policy i've been on mfe's uh interim limits and targets advisor group as well as the mb group that advised um government on the science system under the last group the areas i want to focus on here are the transparency of science and research that tells us how the environment actually is and how it's doing with the statutory functions of the secretary of the environment

and parts that were set up under the original um environment legislation that legislation was sort of world leading at the time and is now the norm to have a ministry for the environment or a similar environmental agency at a top level of government um that shouldn't be a surprise to anyone and bringing people like me to new zealand 25 years ago from ivy league universities in berkeley or simply looking after our trade and international reputation is essential um having the the the role of the ministry for the environment within that's essential i think everybody can see that to some degree the question here is will this bill actually do what it says and what can be done around um making the process here as effective as possible

i i oppose the bill um i oppose um the um this establishment of the ministry for the environment i would have worn a tie if i believe that i could actually convince you to go back on that however i think the more important thing here is something that amongst others the pc has identified there are core things that happen within government and core things that are set up in legislation where the ministry for the environment is relatively unique um my observation is that a lot of those go badly in super ministries and that's certainly what i've observed with the super ministries like mb that have been created i can see benefits from remixing the planning functions of mfv with other ministries i can't see benefit benefits from disestablishing

the roles of mfv as seen in making the more invisible in a super ministry there are two big problems to address one of which that i would really emphasize is making as much as possible here reversible so when it's not seen as working or a different government comes in it's relatively smooth to fix the parts that aren't working and let the parts of the merger that maybe are working continue um one of the most important pieces of those for you to understand is the role of the chief executive and we've now seen a chief executive appointment and that person as has come out in the media just in the last day does not give me confidence that this is going to proceed as planned or was successfully um there's a little more to say about that so what i'll move

to is the problem with ministries you've probably seen this before um i'll certainly leave it as an exhibit this is the unscramble and government diagram from the new zealand initiatives and you know how does science or reality negotiate this i would hand it to you that a lot of the problems we have can be fixed by restructuring but the restructuring might look more like this which is norway and in this model you might see things like climate environment and conservation put together rather than environment as we call it now um put in with um planning and building and transport uh i think there's some very different functions there and i support the pce's notion that these need to have a competition at the table within cabinet

um the last thing that i worry about and i will encourage you to think about my looking around the table i think you can understand this without me explaining it further it's it's mentioned i know or is consistent with the new zealand initiatives unscramble and government report and it's one of the other concerns about restructuring like this that generate super ministries they generate accountability sinks which make government function worse rather than better so with that i think that's really what i wanted to cover and we're close to five minutes anyway yeah thank you troi look we do have a question from francesco cool thank you chair uh can you elaborate on what you mean by how this new ministry could

and he's coming and going you have a problem there we go welcome commissioner and welcome all to the select committee uh you have 10 minutes with us on this so we'll hand it over to you to talk us through your submission so thank you thanks madam chair there's no right way to arrange government departments so anything can be made to work and if you want to get rid of the ministry for the environment this is from a legal point of view an elegant way so that's good however in my view a separate ministry remains the best way to ensure that environmental problems are properly addressed unlike domains such as transport housing or infrastructure where there are numerous private and public owners operators and interest groups

the environment doesn't have anyone that can claim to own it all of us are dependent on the environment of which we're apart now in my view it makes sense to have an agency whose job it is to ensure that the consequences for the environment of all our other policy interventions are properly understood if we can justify a separate ministry for regulation we can certainly justify a separate ministry for the environment now as the officer of parliament charged with keeping under review the system of processes and agencies that make up our environmental management system my advice to you is that this change is unlikely to help the environment and I want to underline three points briefly first bundling these agencies together undermines an

important principle of public sector advice and that is the need for contestability so that elected officials in other words ministers not public servants can confront and make the big trade-offs there's a real risk with a mega ministry that trade-offs will be resolved within the organization and that the differences will not be aired with ministers where accountability for those trade-offs should rightly sit now neither the previous nor the current government have made a convincing case in my view that to reform the rma you need two acts but if you are all agreed that that's the right approach then surely it would make sense to have different ministries responsible for the two different acts to ensure that the trade-offs between

them are properly presented frankly I don't have a lot of confidence in large internal bureaucratic trade-off exercises a ministry for the environment overseeing a natural environment act would better ensure free and frank advice than a mega ministry trying to reach a compromise it hopes all of its ministers can swallow there's also a transparency point that you as a select committee should seek advice on right now the ministry for the environment has two appropriations one for environment one for climate you need to find out in this merged ministry if there is going to be an appropriation for those things with my ee which i bring to you once a year my estimate of environmental expenditure i could tell you what's being spent on those items

from the budget i may not be able to do so anywhere in years so easily if this is all just put into a big blender and you can't see what's being spent on the environment okay my second point if you believe that a smaller number of mega ministries is better then you should at least be prepared to consider merging mfe with mpi now that may shock some people and it's not my first preference after all many of our most pressing environmental problems are associated with primary industries like water quality greenhouse gas emissions and erosion so i suppose there could be a problem for free and frank advice about those problems if you put those together but look at it another way the current proposal is to create a very

urban focused ministry it seems to have been the inspiration of people who live in cities and see the environment as being all about cars and houses and urban design and infrastructure but the vast bulk of the natural environment is outside of cities most of new zealand is rural and i think rural people are much closer to the natural environment they work in it forests farms vineyards they all rely directly on the health of the environment and climate and mpi understands that it's got serious technical capabilities in areas like biosecurity and animal health that are much closer to the natural environment than people advising on how many apartments you can squeeze around a bus stop now i worry that this agency won't understand rural

new zealand and the people who live and work there in short the merger facilitated by this bill treats the environment as though it were a branch of planning law it isn't the environment intersects with every corner of public policy which brings me to my third and final point if ever there was a time to avoid an upheaval it's in the middle of a massive legislative reform which you as a committee are overseeing officials will already have their hands full trying to make complex new legislation work and develop national direction across a bewildering array of topics it's the worst time for people to be sizing up new managers and reapplying for their jobs furthermore the government has decided to upend regional councils which will cause even more

uncertainty for the implementation of new law and as i've said in respect to that reform form should follow function the same applies here if there's a need to tinker with the departmental lego set it should happen after new functions have been determined and legislated for so there you are that is my frank advice to you on this initiative well thank you Simon for that very passionate submission look you've opened up a flurry of questions around the table here so look we will start with Rachel well we know that we can always rely on a frank advice thank you but i've just got one question to start with and that is you're talking about this
